seo.title: Creating and Removing DOM Elements in JavaScript - createElement, appendChild, remove | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn to dynamically create and remove HTML elements with JavaScript. Master createElement, appendChild, prepend, insertBefore, remove, replaceWith, DocumentFragment, and innerHTML vs createElement security. 58+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: javascript createElement, appendChild javascript, remove element javascript, dynamic html javascript, insertBefore javascript, replaceWith javascript, DocumentFragment, javascript dom create elements, javascript build list dynamically, XSS innerHTML

content.what:
What Is Dynamic Element Creation?

So far you have learned to select existing HTML elements and modify them. Now it is time to learn how to create brand new elements from scratch using JavaScript and add them to the page. You can also remove elements that are already there.

This is how real web apps work. When you post a comment on YouTube, the comment does not already exist in the HTML -- JavaScript creates a new element, fills it with your text, and inserts it into the page. When you delete a message, JavaScript removes that element from the DOM.

// Create a new paragraph
const p = document.createElement("p");
p.textContent = "I was created by JavaScript!";
p.classList.add("message");

// Add it to the page
document.body.appendChild(p);

// Remove it later
p.remove();

With createElement, appendChild, and remove, you can build entire interfaces dynamically -- lists, cards, tables, comments, notifications -- all from JavaScript.
content.why:
Why Learn to Create and Remove Elements?

1. Real Apps Are Dynamic

Real websites do not have all their content hardcoded in HTML. Data comes from APIs, user input, or databases. JavaScript takes that data and builds the UI on the fly. Every social media feed, e-commerce product grid, and chat application creates elements dynamically.

2. It Completes Your DOM Toolkit

You know how to select elements (Chapter 11) and respond to events (Chapter 12). Creating and removing elements is the third piece. Together, these three skills let you build any interactive feature.

3. It Teaches You About Performance

Creating elements the wrong way can make your page slow. Learning about DocumentFragment and the differences between innerHTML and createElement teaches you to think about performance -- a skill that separates good developers from great ones.

4. Security: innerHTML vs createElement

Using innerHTML with user input is dangerous (XSS attacks). createElement is safe by default because it does not parse HTML strings. Understanding this difference is critical for building secure websites.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. document.createElement()

This creates a new element in memory. It does not appear on the page until you add it somewhere:

const div = document.createElement("div");
const p = document.createElement("p");
const img = document.createElement("img");
const btn = document.createElement("button");

// At this point, these elements exist in memory but are NOT visible on the page

After creating an element, you typically set its content, attributes, and styles before adding it to the page:

const card = document.createElement("div");
card.className = "card";                // Set class
card.id = "student-card";               // Set id
card.textContent = "Aarav's Card";      // Set text
card.style.padding = "20px";            // Set style
card.setAttribute("data-student", "1"); // Set custom attribute

2. Appending Elements

There are several ways to add an element to the page:

const parent = document.getElementById("container");
const child = document.createElement("p");
child.textContent = "New paragraph";

// appendChild: adds as the last child
parent.appendChild(child);

// prepend: adds as the first child
parent.prepend(child);

// append: like appendChild but accepts strings too
parent.append(child);
parent.append("Plain text"); // Can append text directly

// insertBefore: insert before a specific element
const reference = document.getElementById("existing");
parent.insertBefore(child, reference); // child goes before reference

3. Removing Elements

The modern way to remove an element is simply calling .remove() on it:

const element = document.getElementById("old-item");
element.remove(); // Gone from the page

// Older way (still works): removeChild on the parent
const parent = element.parentNode;
parent.removeChild(element);

4. Replacing Elements

replaceWith() replaces an element with a new one:

const oldElement = document.getElementById("old");
const newElement = document.createElement("div");
newElement.textContent = "I replaced the old element!";

oldElement.replaceWith(newElement);
// oldElement is removed, newElement takes its place

5. Building Structured Elements

You can nest createElement calls to build complex structures:

// Build a card with a title and description
const card = document.createElement("div");
card.className = "card";

const title = document.createElement("h3");
title.textContent = "Aarav's Project";

const desc = document.createElement("p");
desc.textContent = "A calculator app built with JavaScript";

card.appendChild(title);
card.appendChild(desc);

document.getElementById("container").appendChild(card);

6. Creating Elements from Data Arrays

A common pattern is looping through data to create elements for each item:

const students = [
  { name: "Aarav", grade: "A" },
  { name: "Priya", grade: "B+" },
  { name: "Rohan", grade: "A+" }
];

const list = document.getElementById("student-list");

students.forEach(function(student) {
  const li = document.createElement("li");
  li.textContent = student.name + " - Grade: " + student.grade;
  list.appendChild(li);
});

7. DocumentFragment for Performance

When adding many elements, each appendChild causes the browser to re-render. A DocumentFragment lets you build a batch of elements off-screen and add them all at once:

const fragment = document.createDocumentFragment();

for (let i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
  const li = document.createElement("li");
  li.textContent = "Item " + i;
  fragment.appendChild(li); // Adds to fragment (off-screen)
}

document.getElementById("list").appendChild(fragment); // One DOM update!

Without a fragment, the browser would re-render 100 times. With a fragment, it re-renders once. For small lists the difference is negligible, but for large lists it matters.

8. innerHTML vs createElement: Security

innerHTML is quick and easy, but it parses strings as HTML. If the string contains user input, it can execute malicious code (XSS):

// DANGEROUS with user input:
const userInput = '<img src=x onerror="alert(document.cookie)">';
container.innerHTML = userInput; // Executes the script!

// SAFE: createElement + textContent
const p = document.createElement("p");
p.textContent = userInput; // Displays as plain text, no execution
container.appendChild(p);

Rule of thumb: use innerHTML only with trusted, developer-controlled content. Use createElement + textContent for anything involving user input.

9. cloneNode()

cloneNode() creates a copy of an existing element. Pass true for a deep clone (includes children):

const original = document.getElementById("template-card");

// Shallow clone (just the element, no children)
const shallow = original.cloneNode(false);

// Deep clone (element + all children)
const deep = original.cloneNode(true);
deep.id = "card-copy"; // Change the id to avoid duplicates
document.body.appendChild(deep);

codeExamples.title: Building a Dynamic Student List
codeExamples.code: Class Roster
codeExamples.explanation: A reusable createStudentElement function builds a complete student row with name, grade, and a delete button. The delete button removes the element using div.remove(). Initial students are rendered from an array, and new students can be added via the form.
codeExamples.output: A student list with name, grade, and a remove button for each. New students can be added from input fields.

codeExamples.title: Comment System (Add and Delete)
codeExamples.code:
Comments

0 comments
codeExamples.explanation: The addComment function creates a complete comment element with author name, timestamp, text, and a delete button. prepend adds new comments at the top (newest first). The delete button removes its comment and updates the count. All user input is set via textContent for XSS safety.
codeExamples.output: A comment section where users can post and delete comments. New comments appear at the top.

codeExamples.title: Card Grid from Data Array
codeExamples.code: Course Catalog
codeExamples.explanation: We loop through a data array and create a card for each course. Each card has an icon, title, description, and a color-coded difficulty level. We use a DocumentFragment to batch all cards together and add them to the DOM in a single operation for better performance.
codeExamples.output: A responsive grid of course cards built entirely from a JavaScript data array, with color-coded difficulty badges.

codeExamples.title: cloneNode for Template-Based Rendering
codeExamples.code: Notification System
codeExamples.explanation: We use an HTML <template> element as a reusable blueprint. template.content.cloneNode(true) creates a deep copy of the template content. We then fill in the text and type class, attach a close handler, and add it to the page. The template itself is never visible.
codeExamples.output: Buttons add colored notifications (blue info, green success, red error). Each notification has an 'x' button to dismiss it.

codeExamples.title: insertBefore and replaceWith
codeExamples.code:
List Operations

  • First item
  • Second item
  • Third item
codeExamples.explanation: prepend adds at the beginning. insertBefore(newEl, referenceEl) places the new element right before the reference. replaceWith swaps one element for another. lastElementChild.remove() removes the last item. All operations update a status message.
codeExamples.output: Buttons demonstrate prepend, insertBefore, replaceWith, and remove operations on a list. A status message shows what happened.

codeExamples.title: innerHTML vs createElement: Security Demo
codeExamples.code:
innerHTML vs createElement Security



innerHTML (parses HTML -- dangerous with user input):

createElement + textContent (safe -- treats as plain text):
codeExamples.explanation: This demo shows the difference between innerHTML and createElement. If the user types <b>Bold</b>, innerHTML renders it as bold text (it parses the HTML). createElement + textContent displays the raw string including the tags. With malicious input like <img src=x onerror=alert(1)>, innerHTML would execute the script, while textContent is safe.
codeExamples.output: Two areas side by side. innerHTML renders HTML tags. createElement + textContent shows them as plain text.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting to Append the Created Element
commonMistakes.error: No error, but the element never appears on the page. It exists only in JavaScript's memory.
commonMistakes.explanation: createElement creates an element in memory. It is NOT visible until you append it to a visible parent using appendChild, prepend, or insertBefore.

commonMistakes.title: Using innerHTML with User Input (XSS Vulnerability)
commonMistakes.error: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): malicious HTML or JavaScript in user input gets executed by the browser.
commonMistakes.explanation: innerHTML parses strings as HTML. User input with script tags or event handlers (like onerror) will execute. createElement + textContent is safe because it never parses HTML.

commonMistakes.title: Appending the Same Element Twice (Moves Instead of Copying)
commonMistakes.error: An element can only exist in one place in the DOM. Appending it elsewhere moves it from the original location.
commonMistakes.explanation: A DOM node can only have one parent. Appending it to a new parent removes it from the old one. To put the same content in two places, use cloneNode(true) to create a copy.

commonMistakes.title: innerHTML += Destroys Event Listeners
commonMistakes.error: The button still appears but clicking it does nothing. innerHTML += re-parses and rebuilds the entire inner HTML, destroying all event listeners on existing elements.
commonMistakes.explanation: innerHTML += reads the current HTML as a string, appends the new string, and re-parses the entire thing. This destroys all existing DOM nodes and their event listeners. Always use appendChild or prepend to add content without destroying existing elements.

summary: document.createElement('tag') creates a new element in memory. It does not appear on the page until you add it to a parent element.
appendChild adds an element as the last child. prepend adds it as the first child. insertBefore(newEl, refEl) places it before a specific element.
element.remove() removes an element from the page. The older way is parentNode.removeChild(element).
element.replaceWith(newElement) replaces an element with a new one in place.
After creating an element, set its content (textContent), attributes (className, id, setAttribute), and styles before appending it.
Loop through data arrays and create elements for each item. This is the pattern used by every modern web application to render dynamic content.
DocumentFragment lets you batch-create elements off-screen and add them all at once, reducing the number of DOM updates for better performance.
innerHTML is convenient but dangerous with user input (XSS). Always use createElement + textContent for user-provided data.
innerHTML += destroys existing event listeners because it rebuilds the entire inner content. Use appendChild instead.
cloneNode(true) creates a deep copy of an element (including children). cloneNode(false) creates a shallow copy (just the element, no children).
The HTML template element provides a reusable, invisible blueprint. Use template.content.cloneNode(true) to create instances of it.

topicSpecific.hint: createElement creates an element, textContent sets its text, appendChild adds it.
topicSpecific.answer: The div now contains a paragraph that says "Hello from JavaScript!".
topicSpecific.explanation: We create a new <p> element, set its text, and append it to the div. The page now shows the text inside the div.

topicSpecific.answer: The container has two paragraphs: "Original" (first) and "New" (second).
topicSpecific.explanation: appendChild adds the new element as the last child. The original paragraph stays in place, and the new one appears after it.

topicSpecific.answer: The container has two paragraphs: "First!" (first) and "Original" (second).
topicSpecific.explanation: prepend adds the new element as the first child, before any existing content.

topicSpecific.hint: remove() removes the element from the DOM.
topicSpecific.answer: The list now contains only "Apple" and "Cherry". "Banana" is removed.
topicSpecific.explanation: element.remove() removes the element from its parent. The Banana li is no longer in the DOM.

topicSpecific.hint: className sets the class, id sets the id.
topicSpecific.answer: A div with text "Card content" appears inside #box. Console logs: card and my-card.
topicSpecific.explanation: We create a div, set its class to "card", id to "my-card", and text to "Card content". After appending, the div is visible. The console confirms the class and id.

topicSpecific.hint: insertBefore places the new element before the reference element.
topicSpecific.answer: The list contains: A, X, B, C (in that order).
topicSpecific.explanation: insertBefore(newLi, target) places "X" right before "B". The final order is A, X, B, C.

topicSpecific.hint: replaceWith swaps the old element for the new one.
topicSpecific.answer: The page shows a green paragraph with text "New text". The old paragraph is gone.
topicSpecific.explanation: replaceWith removes the old element from the DOM and puts the new element in its place. The old element no longer exists on the page.

topicSpecific.hint: children returns element children. firstElementChild and lastElementChild are the first and last.
topicSpecific.explanation: children returns the 3 p elements. firstElementChild is the first p ("One"). lastElementChild is the last p ("Three").

topicSpecific.hint: cloneNode creates an independent copy. Changing the clone does not affect the original.
topicSpecific.explanation: cloneNode(true) creates a deep copy. We change the clone's text to "World" without affecting the original ("Hello"). The box now has 2 children.

topicSpecific.hint: An element can only exist in one place. Appending it again moves it.
topicSpecific.answer: The container has one paragraph: "Only one".
topicSpecific.explanation: A DOM element can only be in one location. Each appendChild moves the same element (it does not copy it). After three appends of the same element, it is just in its final position -- still one element.

topicSpecific.hint: The while loop keeps removing the first child until there are none left.
topicSpecific.explanation: The while loop removes children one by one until there are none left. After the loop, the ul is empty: 0 children and empty innerHTML. Note: firstChild includes text nodes (whitespace), so this removes everything.

topicSpecific.hint: After appending a fragment, its children move to the parent. The fragment becomes empty.
topicSpecific.explanation: Before appending: fragment has 3 children, box has 0. After appending the fragment, its children are transferred to box. Fragment becomes empty (0 children), box now has 3.

topicSpecific.hint: insertBefore(new, reference) places new before reference.
topicSpecific.explanation: children[1] is the "Third" paragraph. insertBefore(second, third) places "Second" right before "Third". Order: First, Second, Third.

topicSpecific.hint: cloneNode(false) copies only the element. cloneNode(true) copies children too.
topicSpecific.explanation: Shallow clone (false): just the p tag with no children, so 0 childNodes and empty textContent. Deep clone (true): copies all children (text node + strong + text node = 3 childNodes) with their text.

topicSpecific.hint: Create an h2, set its text and color, then append it.
topicSpecific.answer: A purple heading that says "Welcome, Aarav!" appears inside the div.
topicSpecific.explanation: We create an h2 element, set its text to "Welcome, Aarav!", set its color to purple (#a855f7), and append it to the div. The heading appears on the page.

topicSpecific.hint: Each li gets a span appended to it with ' [done]'.
topicSpecific.explanation: For each li, we create a span with " [done]" and append it. Each li now has its original text plus the span text. The console shows all text content joined.

mixed.hint: forEach loops through the array and creates an li for each fruit.
mixed.answer: A list with three items: Apple, Banana, Cherry.
mixed.explanation: The forEach loop creates a new li element for each fruit in the array, sets its text, and appends it to the ul. The list is built dynamically from the data.

mixed.hint: childElementCount tells you how many child elements exist. innerHTML = '' empties the element.
mixed.explanation: Initially the div has 2 child elements (two p tags). Setting innerHTML = "" removes all content. Now childElementCount is 0.

mixed.hint: Each student gets a div with a bold name and a score span.
mixed.answer: Three divs appear: "Aarav - Score: 95", "Priya - Score: 88", "Rohan - Score: 72".
mixed.explanation: For each student, we create a div containing a strong element (name) and a span element (score). Each div is appended to the container.

mixed.question: What is the difference between appendChild and prepend?
mixed.hint: Think about where the new element is placed.
mixed.answer: appendChild adds an element as the last child of the parent. prepend adds it as the first child. Both add the element inside the parent, but at different positions.
mixed.explanation: If a parent has children [A, B, C]: appendChild(D) makes it [A, B, C, D]. prepend(D) makes it [D, A, B, C].

mixed.question: Why is innerHTML += problematic when existing elements have event listeners?
mixed.hint: Think about what happens to the existing elements.
mixed.answer: innerHTML += reads the current HTML as a string, appends the new content, and then completely re-parses and rebuilds all the inner HTML. This destroys the original DOM nodes along with their event listeners. The elements look the same but they are actually new elements without any listeners. Use appendChild or prepend to preserve existing elements and their listeners.
mixed.explanation: Example: a button with a click listener inside the container. After container.innerHTML += '<p>New</p>', the button appears but clicking it does nothing because it is a brand new button element without the listener.

mixed.question: What is a DocumentFragment and when should you use it?
mixed.hint: Think about performance when adding many elements.
mixed.answer: A DocumentFragment is an invisible, lightweight container that exists only in memory. You can add many elements to a fragment without triggering DOM re-renders. When you finally append the fragment to the page, all its children are transferred in a single operation. Use it when creating many elements in a loop (like 100+ list items) to improve performance.
mixed.explanation: Without a fragment, each appendChild triggers a browser re-render. With a fragment, you batch all additions into one re-render. The fragment itself is not added to the DOM -- only its children are.

mixed.hint: An element can only exist in one parent. Appending it elsewhere moves it.
mixed.explanation: After first appendChild: p is in div#a (1 child in a, 0 in b). After second appendChild: p is moved to div#b (0 in a, 1 in b). An element can only have one parent.

mixed.hint: querySelectorAll captures all .remove elements, then forEach removes each one.
mixed.answer: The list shows three "Keep" items. Console logs: 3.
mixed.explanation: querySelectorAll finds both .remove elements. forEach removes each one. The 3 "Keep" items remain (3 children).

mixed.hint: append() can take multiple arguments, including strings.
mixed.explanation: append() can accept multiple arguments including elements and strings. It adds them all in order. The result is two p elements with the text " and " between them.

mixed.hint: After remove(), the element still exists in memory but has no parent.
mixed.explanation: After remove(), the element is detached from the DOM (box has 0 children). But the JavaScript variable p still references the element in memory. Its textContent is still "Hello" but its parentNode is null.

mixed.hint: appendChild adds at the end, prepend adds at the beginning.
mixed.answer: The ul contains: Second (first), then First (second).
mixed.explanation: li1 ("First") is appended at the end. li2 ("Second") is prepended at the beginning. Final order: Second, First.

mixed.hint: insertBefore moves existing elements, not copies them.
mixed.explanation: insertBefore moves C before the first child (A). C is removed from its original position and placed first. New order: C, A, B.

mixed.hint: Template content is not rendered in the DOM. It exists only in the template's content property.
mixed.explanation: The template's innerHTML contains the 'card' string (true). template.content is a DocumentFragment (true). But querySelectorAll('.card') finds 0 elements because template content is not part of the active DOM.

mixed.hint: Setting innerHTML destroys old elements and creates new ones.
mixed.explanation: Setting innerHTML destroys the old p and creates a new one. p1 references the old (detached) element. p2 references the new element. They have the same text but are different objects (p1 === p2 is false).

mixed.question: When should you use innerHTML and when should you use createElement?
mixed.hint: Think about security, performance, and event listeners.
mixed.answer: Use innerHTML when: (1) you need to set a large chunk of trusted, static HTML quickly, and (2) the content does not come from user input. Use createElement when: (1) the content includes user input (for XSS safety), (2) existing elements have event listeners you want to preserve, and (3) you need references to the created elements for further manipulation.
mixed.explanation: innerHTML is faster for bulk HTML insertion but destroys existing elements and their listeners, and is unsafe with user input. createElement is safer, preserves existing DOM, and gives you direct references to new elements.

mcqs.question: Which method creates a new HTML element?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. document.createElement('tag') creates a new element. The other methods do not exist in the DOM API.

mcqs.question: Where does appendChild place the new element?

options.B: After all children (as the last child)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. appendChild adds the new element as the last child of the parent. To add as the first child, use prepend.

mcqs.question: How do you remove an element from the DOM?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. element.remove() removes the element from the DOM. delete and destroy are not DOM methods. hide does not exist (you would use CSS for hiding).

mcqs.question: What happens if you call createElement but never appendChild?

options.A: The element appears at the bottom of the page
options.C: The element exists in memory but does not appear on the page
options.D: The element is automatically appended to body

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. createElement creates an element in JavaScript's memory. It has no parent and is invisible. You must explicitly append it to a visible element for it to appear.

mcqs.question: What does element.replaceWith(newElement) do?

options.B: Removes element and puts newElement in its place

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. replaceWith removes the original element from the DOM and inserts the new element exactly where the old one was.

mcqs.question: What is the danger of using innerHTML with user input?

options.B: It does not work in all browsers
options.C: It can cause XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. innerHTML parses strings as HTML. If user input contains malicious scripts (like <script> tags or event handlers), they get executed. Always use textContent for user input.

mcqs.question: What happens when you append the same element to a different parent?

options.A: It is copied to the new parent
options.B: It is moved from the old parent to the new parent

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. A DOM element can only have one parent. Appending it to a new parent moves it from the old one. To have it in both places, use cloneNode(true).

mcqs.question: What does cloneNode(true) do compared to cloneNode(false)?

options.A: true makes a deep copy (including children), false makes a shallow copy (element only)
options.B: true makes it visible, false keeps it hidden
options.C: true clones styles, false does not

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. cloneNode(true) creates a deep clone: the element AND all its children and their text. cloneNode(false) clones only the element itself, with no children.

mcqs.question: Why does innerHTML += destroy event listeners on existing elements?

options.B: It reads innerHTML as a string, appends text, then re-parses everything -- creating new elements without listeners
options.D: Event listeners expire after innerHTML changes

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. innerHTML += serializes current content to a string, concatenates the new string, then re-parses the entire result. The old DOM nodes (with their listeners) are destroyed and replaced with new ones that look the same but have no listeners.

mcqs.question: What is a DocumentFragment?

options.A: A broken part of the DOM
options.C: A lightweight, invisible container for batching DOM operations
options.D: A cached copy of the page

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A DocumentFragment is a minimal document object that is not part of the active DOM tree. You can add elements to it without triggering re-renders, then append the fragment to add all elements at once.

mcqs.question: Which method adds an element as the first child of a parent?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. prepend adds an element at the beginning (first child). appendChild adds at the end. insertBefore can also achieve this but requires a reference to the current first child.

mcqs.question: What does the append method accept that appendChild does not?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. append() can take multiple arguments and accepts both elements and strings. appendChild() accepts only a single node and returns the appended node.

mcqs.question: After calling element.remove(), what happens to JavaScript variables referencing that element?

options.C: They still reference the element in memory, but it has no parent
options.D: They throw an error when accessed

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. remove() detaches the element from the DOM, but the JavaScript variable still references the element object in memory. Its parentNode becomes null, but you can still read its properties or even re-append it.

mcqs.question: What does insertBefore(newNode, null) do?

options.C: Inserts at the end (same as appendChild)

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. When the reference node is null, insertBefore appends the new node at the end of the parent's children, behaving exactly like appendChild.

mcqs.question: What does element.textContent = '' do?

options.A: Removes the element from the DOM
options.B: Removes all text and child elements inside the element
options.C: Sets the text to the word 'empty'

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Setting textContent to an empty string removes all child nodes (text and elements) from inside the element. The element itself remains in the DOM, just empty.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between append() and appendChild()?

options.A: append can take strings and multiple arguments; appendChild takes only one node

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. append() can accept multiple arguments including strings and nodes. appendChild() takes only a single DOM node and returns the appended node.

mcqs.question: What does parentNode return for an element that has been removed with remove()?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. After calling remove(), the element is detached from the DOM. Its parentNode becomes null because it no longer has a parent.

mcqs.question: Why is DocumentFragment better than appending elements one by one in a loop?

options.A: Fragments are visible while being built
options.B: Each individual appendChild triggers a browser re-render; the fragment batches them into one

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Each appendChild call on a visible element can trigger the browser to re-render. A DocumentFragment is an invisible container; building elements there does not trigger renders. Appending the fragment causes just one re-render.

mcqs.question: What method creates a text node (not an element)?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. document.createTextNode('some text') creates a text node. This is rarely needed because textContent is simpler, but it is useful for precise text insertion.

codingChallenges.title: Dynamic Shopping List
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with an input field and an 'Add Item' button. When the button is clicked, add the input text as a new list item. Each list item should have a 'Remove' button that deletes it. Show the total count of items above the list.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: List item 'Milk' appears with a remove button. Count shows '1 item'.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use createElement, appendChild, remove. Use textContent for the item text.
codingChallenges.solution:
Shopping List

0 items

codingChallenges.title: Student Cards from Array
codingChallenges.description: Given an array of student objects [{name, age, city}], create a card for each student and add them to the page. Each card should show the name (bold), age, and city. Use a DocumentFragment for performance.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Three styled cards appear on the page with student info.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use createElement, DocumentFragment, textContent. Do not use innerHTML.
codingChallenges.solution: Students

codingChallenges.title: Notification Stack
codingChallenges.description: Build a notification system. Add buttons for 'Info', 'Success', 'Warning', and 'Error' notifications. Each notification appears at the top of a stack, shows a message and type, has a close button, and auto-removes after 5 seconds. Use different background colors for each type.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A green notification appears saying 'Success: Operation completed!' with an 'x' button. It disappears after 5 seconds.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use createElement, prepend, remove, setTimeout. Use classList for type-based styling.

codingChallenges.title: Sortable List with Move Up/Down
codingChallenges.description: Create a list of 5 items. Each item has 'Move Up' and 'Move Down' buttons. Clicking 'Move Up' moves the item one position up in the list. Clicking 'Move Down' moves it one position down. The first item should not move up, and the last should not move down.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: Click 'Move Up' on the third item
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: The third item moves to the second position.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use insertBefore for moving. Check if the element is first or last before moving.
codingChallenges.solution:
Reorder List

  • Item 1
  • Item 2
  • Item 3
  • Item 4
  • Item 5

codingChallenges.title: Dynamic Table Builder
codingChallenges.description: Build a table generator. The user enters the number of rows and columns. When they click 'Generate', create a table with those dimensions. Each cell should show its row and column number (e.g., 'R1C2'). Add a 'Delete Row' button at the end of each row and a 'Delete Column' button at the top of each column.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A 3x4 table with cells labeled R1C1, R1C2, etc. Delete buttons on rows and columns.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use createElement for table, tr, td, th elements. Use event delegation for delete buttons.
codingChallenges.solution:
Table Builder

rows cols

codingChallenges.title: Bookmark Manager
codingChallenges.description: Build a bookmark manager. Users can add bookmarks with a title and URL. Each bookmark is displayed as a card with the title (as a clickable link), URL text, and a 'Delete' button. Add a search input that filters bookmarks by title in real time. Use textContent for the title (for XSS safety) and setAttribute for the href.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A bookmark card appears with 'Google' as a clickable link and a delete button.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use createElement for everything. Use textContent for user input. Use setAttribute for href. Use a DocumentFragment when initially rendering.
codingChallenges.solution: Bookmarks
